1: Standard word-event formula introduces the qinah (lament) over Tyre. The chapter is entirely composed as a funeral song in the second person, addressed to Tyre directly.
2: veʾattah ben-ʾadam seʾa ʿal-Tsur qinah — "and you, son of man, take up a lament over Tyre" — the command to raise a qinah is the standard introduction to the dirge genre (also 19:1; 26:17; 28:12; 32:2). The qinah meter (3+2 beats) creates the limping, mournful rhythm associated with death throughout the Hebrew poetic tradition.
3: Tyre's self-description — ʾani kelilat yofi — "I am perfect in beauty" — echoes the pride-language of the Eden-cherub in 28:12. The pride of Tyre is her maritime position: yoshevet ʿal-mevoʾot yam (you who sit at the gateway to the sea) and her role as merchant to many peoples (rohelet haʿammim). The term rohelet (female merchant/trader, from rahal, to trade) personifies the city as a woman engaged in commerce.
4: The ship metaphor begins — bigvul yammim bonayikh kallelu yofeikh — "your builders perfected your beauty in the midst of the seas" — bigvul yammim (in the heart/boundary of the seas) is Tyre's geographic location on an island. The builders (bonayikh) who perfected her beauty are those who built the physical city; the beauty metaphor applied to a city's architecture is common in wisdom poetry.
5: The shipbuilding details begin with the timber — ʿatsei shenim mizLevanon laʾsu lakh leʾoren lehashpilat kanafeikh — "they made all your planks of fir trees from Lebanon; they took a cedar from Lebanon to make a mast for you." The sourcing of cedar and fir from Lebanon is historically accurate — the Phoenicians were the primary timber traders of the ancient world, using Lebanese cedar for their own shipbuilding and export.
6: The oars from oaks of Bashan — allonim miBashan ʿasu mishshoteikh qarsheikh ʿasu shen-bat-ʾashshurim meʾiyyei Kittim — "of oaks of Bashan they made your oars; your deck they made of ivory inlaid in boxwood from the coasts of Cyprus." Bat-ʾashshurim is unclear — possibly "daughter of Asshur-trees" (a species of cypress) or a corrupt text. Kittim consistently refers to Cyprus in the OT.
7: The sail of Egypt — shesh-beriqmah miMitsrayim hayah mifrasek lihyot lakh nes — "fine embroidered linen from Egypt was your sail, serving as your banner" — shesh (fine linen) is the high-quality Egyptian linen used for priestly garments and for luxury sails. The awning materials from Elishah (probably Alashiya = Cyprus) complete the luxury ship-fitting.
8: The professional crew — yoshevei Tsidon veʾArvad hayu shotatayikh ḥakhameikh Tsur hayu vakh yoshevei ḥavveleikh — sailors from Sidon and Arvad (Arwad, an island city north of Byblos), skilled craftsmen and pilots from Tyre's own population. The ḥakhamim (wise men/skilled craftsmen) of Tyre are the same class referenced in 28:3-5 — the expertise of the city is built into both the trade-ship lament and the king-of-Tyre oracle.
9: The elders of Gebal (= Byblos) as caulkers — ziqnei Geval vaḥakhameyhah hayu vakh maḥazeqei bidzqeikh kol-ʾaniyot hayam veshallehem hayu vakh laʿarov markolteikh — Gebal/Byblos was a Phoenician city whose elders were skilled shipwrights (ḥazaq = to strengthen/caulk). The phrase "all the ships of the sea and their mariners were in you to trade your merchandise" summarizes Tyre's function as the trading hub of the ancient world.
10: Persia, Lud (Lydia), and Put in Tyre's army — Paras veLud vaPhut hayu veḥeileikh ʾanshei milḥamateikh — "Persia and Lud and Put were in your army, your men of war" — the foreign military professionals who served as mercenaries. Phut (Libya/Somalia) and Lud (Lydia in western Anatolia) indicate the geographic breadth of Tyre's military recruitment. The hanging of shields and helmets on walls (hem tallu ʿaleikh tsinnah vekhovaʿ hem natenu hoddekha) is the decorative military display typical of Phoenician cities.
11: The Arvadites and Gammadites on the towers — uvenei Arvad veḥeileikh ʿal-ḥomotayikh saviv vegammadim bemigdolotayikh hayu ʾashperoteyhem tillu ʿal-ḥomotayikh saviv hem killu yofeikh — the Arvadites as garrison troops on the walls, Gammadim (origin uncertain — possibly a place-name or a term for elite warriors) on the towers. The decoration of the city with military hardware ("they completed/perfected your beauty") connects back to the kelilat yofi (perfect in beauty) claim of v3.
12: The trade catalog begins with Tarshish (Tarshish sokhartekh merov kol-hon bekhesef barzel bedia uvol natan ʿizaveikh) — Tarshish (probably Tartessus in Spain) trading silver, iron, tin, and lead for Tyre's wares. The verb ʿizzevikh (supplied you) from ʿazav (leave/leave for, here in the sense of leaving goods in payment) will recur as the structural verb of the trade catalog throughout the chapter.
13: Greece (Javan), Tubal, and Meshech — Yavan Tuval uMeshekh hemmah rohaleikh benefshet ʾadam ukhle neḥoshet natenu ʿizbeikh — trading in human slaves (nefesh ʾadam, lit. human soul/person — the OT term for slave trade) and bronze vessels. Javan (Ionia/Greece), Tubal and Meshech (regions in Anatolia/Caucasus) are grouped together as they are in the Table of Nations (Gen 10:2) and in the Gog oracle (Ezek 38-39).
14: Beth-togarmah (mivet Togarmah susim uperashim uperedim natenu ʿizbeikh) — trading horses, war-horses, and mules. Togarmah (probably Armenia/southeastern Anatolia) was known in antiquity for its horse-breeding; the connection is confirmed by Assyrian annals that record tribute in horses from that region.
15: The sons of Dedan — benei Dedan rohalaikh iyyim rabbim sekullat yadeikh shenhabbim vehobnin heshivu ʿeshbeikh — trading from "many coastlands" in ivory (shen-ʾabbim, lit. elephant-tooth) and ebony (hobnin, the word is borrowed from an Egyptian or Semitic root for hard dark wood). Dedan (northwest Arabia) served as a caravan hub connecting Arabian and African trade goods to Mediterranean markets.
16: Aram (Syria) — Aram sokhartekh merov maʿaseikh benovfeikh veʾargavan veriqmah uvuts veraʾmot venokhet natenu ʿizbeikh — trading turquoise (nofekh), purple (ʾargaman), embroidered work (riqmah), fine linen (buts), coral (raʾmot), and rubies/red coral (kadkod — the identification is uncertain). The luxuries are consistent with what Aram controlled through caravan routes.
17: Judah and Israel — Yehudah veʾerets Yisraʾel hemmah rohaleikh beḥitey Minnit vepannat udvaish vashemen utsori natenu maʿarveikh — trading wheat of Minnith (a place in Ammon, Judg 11:33), confections (pannat — uncertain, possibly pastry/cakes), honey, oil, and balm (tsori, the resin product associated with Gilead). The inclusion of Judah and Israel in the trade catalog is significant: even YHWH's covenant people are commercial partners of Tyre.
18: Damascus — Dammesek sokhartekh berov maʿaseikh merov kol-hon beyein Ḥelbon uvetsemer Tsaḥar — trading Helbon wine (a premium wine region near Damascus) and white wool (tsemer Tsaḥar or wool of Zahar). The wine of Helbon was noted in Assyrian records as a luxury item sent to the royal court.
19: Dan and Javan and Uzal — vedan veYavan meʾuzzal beʾizbeikh natanu barzel ʿashut qinnamon uqane hayah bemaʿarveikh — Dan and Javan (possibly Aden or a Yemenite location named Uzal/Azal; Gen 10:27) trading wrought iron, cassia, and cane. The aromatic spices (cassia, cane) were luxury goods from the East.
20: Dedan — Dedan rohaltekh bebigdei ḥofshy lerikbah — trading in saddle blankets/riding cloths (bigdei ḥofshi lerikbah). Dedan's second appearance in the catalog (also v15) reflects its dual role as both an ivory/ebony importer and a textile supplier.
21: Arabia and all the princes of Kedar — Araviʾah vekhol neisiʾei Qedar hemmah sokherei yadeikh bekevashim uʾeilim vaʿattudim bam sokhartekh — trading in lambs (kevasim), rams (ʾeilim), and goats (ʿattudim). The pastoral/livestock trade of the Arabian peninsula rounds out the catalog's commodity diversity.
22: The merchants of Sheba and Raamah — rohallei Shevaʾ veRaʿmah hemmah rohalaikh beroshe kol-boshem uvekhal-ʾeven yeqarah uvezahav natanu ʿizbeikh — trading spices (beshem) and precious stones and gold. Sheba (southwest Arabia/Ethiopia) was associated with gold, spices, and precious stones in the OT (1 Kgs 10:2; Ps 72:10; Isa 60:6).
23: Haran, Canneh, Eden, the merchants of Sheba, Asshur, and Chilmad — Ḥaran veKhanneh veʿEden rohallei Shevaʾ Ashshur veKhilmad rohaltekh — the northern Mesopotamian trade centers complete the circle. Haran (the city of Abraham's sojourn) and Asshur (Assyria) appear as commercial partners alongside the south Arabian Sheba, indicating the full compass range of Tyre's trade network.
24: These traders bring maklulim — "complete/choice things" — including luxurious garments (gelomei tekhelet verigmah), treasures of fine cloths (geneizei brumim), and bound and bound-and-twisted cords (vaḥavlim veʾarozim bemarkolloteikh). The vocabulary here is highly specialized and several terms are hapax legomena (words occurring only here). The textile and cord trades may reflect particular Phoenician specializations.
25: ʾaniyot Tarshish sharat suḥrateikh vatimmaʾli vatikked meʾod belev yammim — "the ships of Tarshish were carriers for your merchandise, and you were filled and made glorious in the heart of the seas." The trade catalog concludes with the aniyyot Tarshish (Tarshish-ships — a class of large ocean-going cargo vessels rather than necessarily ships bound for Tarshish specifically). Their "singing" (sharat) Tyre's trade is a personification of commercial activity.
26: The east-wind destruction — beʿaddeikh heshiʾahu shotatayikh ʾet-yam hamereḥet ruaḥ haqqadim shevaratikh belev yammim — "your rowers have brought you into deep waters; the east wind has wrecked you in the heart of the seas." The ruaḥ haqqadim (east wind) is the hot, destructive wind from the Arabah/desert that destroys ships and crops throughout the OT (Jonah 4:8; Hos 13:15; Ps 48:7). The east wind from YHWH is the agent of Tyre's destruction after the elaborate building of the ship in vv4-25.
27: The sinking inventory — honayikh veʾozaroteikh maʿaraveikh ḥalatayikh veʾanshei milḥamateikh umisraʿim rabbim bekol-qahal betokhekh yamafilu belev yammim beyom mappaltek — every element of Tyre's wealth and personnel enumerated in the trade catalog now sinks: riches, merchandise, exchange goods, mariners, pilots, repairers, traders, warriors, all the assembly. The inverted echo of the catalog is the literary conclusion of the ship-metaphor.
28: The sailors on the land shake at the sound of the cry of the pilots — liqol zeʿaqat ḥavvalaikh yirʿashu migrashot — the trembling coastal communities (migrashot, lit. open spaces/suburbs outside a city) echo ch 26's description of the coastlands trembling at Tyre's fall.
29: The abandonment of the ships — veyardu meʾaniyyoteyhem kol tofssei mishet ḥalatayim vekhol-ʾanshei hayam el-haʾarets yaʿamodu — everyone who works the sea (ships' crews) comes ashore. The movement from ship to land in response to Tyre's fall inverts Tyre's perpetual habitation of the sea.
30: The mourning postures — vehishmiʿu ʿaleikh beqolam vayitsʿaqu mara veyaallu ʿafar ʿal-rosheiyhem beʾefer yitpalashu — raising the voice in bitter crying (mara), putting dust on the head (ʿafar ʿal-rosheiyhem), rolling in ashes (beʾefer yitpalashu) — three mourning gestures. Hitpalesh (roll in ashes/dust) appears also in Jer 6:26 and Mic 1:10 for intense mourning.
31: Shaving the head and girding with sackcloth — vehiqriḥu ʾaleyikh qorḥah veḥagru saqim uvaḥu ʾeleyikh bemarrat nefesh mourning bitter of soul — the combination of shaved head (qorḥah), sackcloth (saq), and bitter weeping (marat nefesh) is the standard mourning-triad of the OT. The mourning is performed specifically ʿaleikh (over/for you, Tyre).
32: The qinah (dirge) composed by the mourners — unesʾu ʿaleikh benihem qinah veqanenu ʿalaykh mi keTsur kedomemah betokh hayam — "and in their wailing they raise a lamentation for you and lament over you: Who is like Tyre, like her who is stilled in the midst of the sea?" — the rhetorical mi-k (who is like?) formula is the OT incomparability formula applied here to commercial greatness. The same form appears in the Exodus songs (Exod 15:11, mi-khamokha) where YHWH's incomparability is declared. Here the incomparability is Tyre's commercial power — which makes its loss all the more devastating.
33: The goods flowing out from Tyre satisfying many peoples — betset ʿizbeikh miyyammim hisbaʿta ʿammim rabbim verov honayikh umaʿaraveikh hivʿarta malkei ʾerets — "when your wares came from the seas you satisfied many peoples; with your abundance of wealth and merchandise you enriched the kings of the earth." The verb hisbaʿta (you satisfied/filled) and hivʿarta (you enriched, lit. made rich) are the economic vocabulary of Tyre's commercial power at its height — now mourned as past.
34: The time of breaking — ʿet nishbaret miyyammim bemaʿaqqei mayim maʿaraveikh ukhol-qahalekh betokh nafalu — "now you are wrecked by the seas, in the depths of the waters; your merchandise and all your crew have sunk with you." The temporal shift from past prosperity (v33) to present destruction (ʿet, now/at the time) creates the rhetorical pathos of the lament.
35: The horror of the coastlands — kol yoshevei haʾiyyim nashemu ʿaleikh umalkeyhem saʿaru saʿar raʿu phanim — the same aghast-reaction vocabulary of 26:15-18 is repeated here to frame the lament from the mourner's perspective. Saʿaru saʿar (their hair bristles in terror — cognate with seʿar, hair) is an idiomatic expression for extreme fear.
36: The hissing conclusion — sokhrim baʿammim sherequ ʿalaykh ballahot hayit veʾeynekh leʿolam ʿad — "the merchants among the peoples hiss at you; you have come to a dreadful end and shall be no more forever." Sharequ (hissing) is both the sound of shock and the ritual gesture of contempt/dismissal for a destroyed city (Jer 19:8; 49:17; Lam 2:15). Ballahot hayit (you have become terrors) echoes the ballahot conclusion of 26:21 — the same word closing both the oracle and the lament.
